chief_complaint: A 35-year-old man presented with acute chest pain.
history: The patient had no prior history of renal impairment, cardiovascular disease, or risk factors such as hypertension or diabetes. He was diagnosed with acute ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) and subsequently evaluated for membranous nephropathy (MN) with nephrotic-range proteinuria.
exam: Vital signs and physical examination findings not provided.
labs: 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) demonstrated ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and augmented vector foot, consistent with inferior-wall myocardial infarction. Emergency coronary angiography showed acute occlusion of the RCA. Blood tests revealed nephrotic-range proteinuria and hypoalbuminemia.
correct_diagnosis: Coronary thrombosis secondary to a nephrotic syndrome-associated hypercoagulable state
differentials: Coronary thrombosis secondary to a nephrotic syndrome-associated hypercoagulable state
Acute coronary occlusion due to rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque
Acute myocardial infarction due to hypertension
Acute myocardial infarction due to diabetes
correct_ranking: Coronary thrombosis secondary to a nephrotic syndrome-associated hypercoagulable state
Acute coronary occlusion due to rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque
Acute myocardial infarction due to hypertension
Acute myocardial infarction due to diabetes
